The city is also the assembly location for vehicles such as the first-generation Ford Mustang, the second-generation Ford Escort (Europe), and the Mark II Ford Cortina. The film "Letter to America" was produced in Amsterdam, and music by Armin van Buuren, such as "Drowning" and "Rush Hour," as well as "Not Giving Up on Love," was recorded there. The play "Anne" premiered in Amsterdam. The city also hosts events and institutions such as the 1996 Johan Cruyff Shield, the Amsterdam University of the Arts, Ajax Magazine, and De Roode Duivel.

Amsterdam has served as a constituency district for Theodorus Aaninck. Works such as "The Social Contract" and the Leesmuseum voor Vrouwen are associated with the country of Amsterdam. The city is a destination for airlines including Helios Airways, Sierra Leone Airways, and Air Atlas Express. Tradus was founded in Amsterdam, and organizations such as Aflatoun and the Kurdistan National Congress (Mrs. Zeyneb Morad and Mr. Ahmet Karamûs) were founded by or in the city.

Several artists and bands, including Aux Raus, Loïs Lane, and Area21, have Amsterdam as their hometown. Individuals such as Eduard Willem Walch, Graham Lees, and Birgit Kos are affiliated with institutions or agencies in Amsterdam. Notable alumni of Amsterdam institutions include Frank Scheffer, Stephan Brenninkmeijer, Mochtar Apin, Peter Spier, and Hendrik Arent Hamaker. People such as Niek van Dijk have been employed in Amsterdam, while Marc Bauer, Albert Muis, Monika Sosnowska, Benjamin Prins, and Hans Bouman have received training there. Til Brugman has received an award in Amsterdam, and Malakai Black and Wim Ruska have been billed from the city. Athletes such as Jan Wienese, Hans Muller, Nico Lutkeveld, Anton Toscani, Sjaak Swart, and Derek Agyakwa have played for clubs or teams in Amsterdam. Julius Wolff (mathematician) has a child in Amsterdam, Maup Caransa is known for his association with the city, and Super Lemon Haze originated in Amsterdam.

Amsterdam is home to diverse populations, including Jewish, Saint Thomas Christian, Surinamese, Japanese, and Iraqi communities in the Netherlands. Residents such as Jacob Kohnstamm, Niels Lootsma, and Abraham Coster live in Amsterdam, while Herman Heijermans, Johannes Elison, Titus van Rijn, Harry Mulisch, and Marga Minco are buried there.

Historically, Amsterdam has been the largest city of the Batavian Republic and has served as the capital for the Dutch government-in-exile, the Netherlands-Indonesia Union, the Reichskommissariat Niederlande, and the United Kingdom of the Netherlands. The city was the site of significant events such as the Bijltjesoproer, the Anabaptist riot, and the Attack on Amsterdam in 1650.
